paper_title: Flora Visitation by Tetragonisca angustula in Rio de Janeiro

methods.description: This method involves in-situ sampling of flowers and capturing insects to study the flora visited by Tetragonisca angustula. The purpose is to observe and capture bees visiting flowers to understand their floral preferences.

steps.description: Sampling started in March 2008 and finished in February 2009. For observation and captures in the flowers, bimonthly sampling was done in four sites chosen at random, one in each region, six hours per day (6:00 to 12:00h, period of highest visitation of bees in the forest, totalizing a schedule 30 hours per site).
steps.searchable_summary: Conduct bimonthly field sampling of bee visitation at four sites over a year

steps.description: Sampling was done at each site within a delimited restricted area of about 8-10m from the forest edges and over 400m of length, and this same area was analyzed each sampling day.
steps.searchable_summary: Define and sample a restricted area for bee visitation

steps.description: With the use of insect nets, T. angustula was captured when observed in flowering plants, considering the overall abundance as much as possible.
steps.searchable_summary: Capture T. angustula using insect nets during flower visitation

steps.description: Plant species were considered attractive to bees when they were visited by more than five bees during a particular time interval (10min).
steps.searchable_summary: Determine plant attractiveness based on bee visitation frequency

steps.description: The plants were prepared as vouchers and identified with the help of specialized literature, other collections and scientists at Universities.

methods.description: This method involves collecting pollen loads and honey from T. angustula hives to identify the plants visited by the bees. The analysis helps in determining the botanical species composition and pollen spectrum.

steps.description: Over the year, pollen loads were collected in five T. angustula hives belonging to a beekeeper located in one of the studied regions (Ilha Grande).
steps.searchable_summary: Collect pollen loads from T. angustula hives

steps.description: During the honey flow, samples of 10g were collected directly from the pots with a syringe.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect honey samples during honey flow

steps.description: Pollen and honey analysis were carried out using the direct methodology (Louveaux et al. 1970) and acetolysis (Erdtman 1960).
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze pollen and honey using direct methodology and acetolysis

steps.description: Qualitative analysis of pollen grains allowed the determination of botanical species that make up the pollen spectrum.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform qualitative analysis to determine botanical species in pollen spectrum

steps.description: Through quantitative analysis of the pollen grains, it was possible to establish the amount of pollen supplied by each floral species, and the frequency when compared with other plants of their diet.
steps.searchable_summary: Conduct quantitative analysis to establish pollen supply and frequency

steps.description: Photomicrographs of pollen grains were also obtained and catalogued.
steps.searchable_summary: Obtain and catalog photomicrographs of pollen grains

steps.description: The polar axis (P) and equatorial diameter (E) of 25 pollen grains were measured; other measurements, such as the apertures, endoapertures and thickness of the exine, were taken in for 10 pollen grains.
steps.searchable_summary: Measure polar axis, equatorial diameter, and other features of pollen grains

steps.description: To calculate the mean of each parameter, we used the arithmetic mean with the standard deviation method.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate mean and standard deviation of pollen measurements

steps.description: The ratio of polar to equatorial axis (P/E) based on the mean, was used for characterization of pollen grains shape.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate P/E ratio for pollen grain shape characterization
